was like the week of Christmas and New Year's.

feel like during that time, it feels like a mini year.

Well, I think this was like I think two and a half years ago, I decided to become a digital nomad.
So basically working and traveling, because I've been working remotely, I think for the last four years now.
So I think that was like just think that was like
An easy transition for me.
What I do like to do is to spend a considerable amount of time in an area.
Because before I was spending like a month and I thought in the end, it felt like it's too much travel, too much logistics to handle.
So right now I'm thinking doing like anything above six months.
So if I really like a place, I stay a little bit longer.

Oh, so right now, okay, so this year, yeah, this gets me excited, actually.
So this year, I'm mostly trying to focus on Africa.

But and I've also got like family and everything in Zambia, because that's where my family was originally from.
So I'm thinking mostly Zambia, so I can go, you know, reconnect.

So I'm trying to do that as well.
That would be awesome.
mean, you could always go to Kenya afterwards, I guess.

And Kigali is amazing.

mandatory.

not even like a bottle on the side of the road.

dollars, but it's half the price in Uganda.

So they're only with humans one hour.
But, like, then you...

then...

So since then, you've been at Cinch?
Yes.
So, yes.
So Cinch purchased the company that I'm working at, Simple Texting.
So Simple Texting is a little bit more straightforward.
So they're a text message provider.
So basically what they do is they provide messages.
So like, you know, when you get that text message from Uber or Google or even the two-factor text messages, that's what we provide.
Ah, okay.
And then what's Cinch?
Why did they buy Simple Texting?
So they're trying to do...
So Cinch is based out of Australia.
And they have operations basically all over the world.
So they do an amalgamation of small business tools.
So they do, you know, marketing, text messages, email marketing, everything.
So they bought Simple Texting as a way to enter the text messaging market in America.
So their big goal is to package everything as a one-stop shop.
So...
order Protocol!
So...
So if you want to do marketing, text messaging, email marketing, all those kinds of things, it'd be a one-stop shop.
I see.
So were you there when they bought it?
When did that happen?
I'll say that happened two years ago now.
So we've only gone through the rebrand now.
So before, we will purchase, but then we were told that we're just going to be on the side and do our own thing.
And then now, basically, we've got a whole rebrand, and then everything is running through Cinch now.
Got it.
I see.
It kind of makes sense why they would purchase it.
They've got voice and video and verification APIs and all that.
Okay.
So tell me about your role there specifically, like maybe before acquisition, and then what's changed since then as well?
Yeah.
I'll say that my role hasn't really changed post-acquisition, but basically I handle all operations.
So I handle all internal operations, so onboarding, training, making sure all our processes are set up, project management as well for any new processes.
So a big aspect of it is also rollout of any new software.
And then on the client side, I do also assist in the project management of client-specific projects.
So those are not as common, but I think I would say like we get maybe, I'll say on average, maybe five or six projects a month.
So I handle the project management of that as well.
What would be an example project?
So an example project depends.
And so like, for example, it would be like, you know, if they're trying to set up a new API.
So sometimes the engineer.
You guys on both sides need to work together.
And then also we do also a little bit of internal marketing so we can get our marketing team just to assist them.
So that usually is an upsell that the guys provide.
Oh, so you offer it to your clients, marketing?
Yeah, it's like an internal, it's more of a little small upsell that we provide to some of the enterprise clients.
So it's not an official product, like you can't see it on the website, but it's something that we do offer internally.
And what would they be, what exactly are you selling?
Oh, so for text messaging and marketing.
So basically we can set them up with a cadence, help them out with a copy, you know, and then get them started.
So usually what we do is that we do the legwork.
So let's say set it up for like three months and then basically hand it over to them so they can move on internally with it.
Okay, so the main product is more like the software.
And then you also offer sort of like an implementation program or something as well.
That's kind of the upsell?
Yes.
Yes.
So we offer, I think implementation is the best way to describe it.
So we offer the implementation to set them up so that they can get used to the processes.
And then also on the other hand, the other projects we offer is like, you know, if you want to get API integration with all their systems.
So depending on the complexity of the two stack, it may vary on how long that project may take.
Okay.
I see.
And then how many people work in the company?
Oh, we've been growing quite quickly, actually.
I would say we're around 200 right now, but less than 300, I would think.
In Simply Texting or in Cinch?
No, just Simple Texting.
Okay.
I'm looking at the LinkedIn and it looks like it's shrunk from like 90 to 50.
I guess maybe people don't.

How many customers do you have, like, approximately?
Like, I'm just trying to gauge, it, like, big ACVs, less customers, or do you have, like, a lot of customers, but it's smaller spend for customers?
So, of course, there's two sides of the business.
There's the self-service side, where you're going to see, you know, your mom-and-pop shop signing up for, like, $50 a month.
Okay.
Yes.
And then on the other side, we've got our enterprise clients.
I would say if we're looking at customer numbers, I would say maybe around 1,000.
But then, of course, the bulk of those will be those small self-service clients.
So if you're looking at the big money clients, I would say you're looking at around $250,000, $300,000.
right.
Okay, that's pretty good.

I have just no idea.
It depends.
depends.
So for us, Enterprise starts at $1,500 a month.
Okay.
How do you bill?
What's the pricing based on?
Is it volume?
Yes.
So the pricing is based on volume.
So what they do is that you basically purchase credits.
So, yeah.
And then, like, each credit is a text message that can be sent out.
And then, of course, like, if you're sending an MMS, which is like a picture message, that will cost you a little bit more.
So that costs you three credits.
So it's basically based on usage.
Okay, got it, got it.
Okay, cool.
Can you walk me through, like, one or two examples of, like, like, larger projects you've run lately?
Yeah, I think.
think.
One of my favorite ones, which was also a little bit of a headache, was when I first joined Simple Texting.
So they didn't really have an operations person.
So they had the similar startup personality of like, oh, we just need to get things done.
We're just running, trying to focus on growth.
So the teams were quite isolated.
Each team used a different messaging service.
So I remember the engineers preferred only to use Google Chat.
Surprisingly, I do not know why.
And then other people were using Teams.
So my first project when I saw that was like, we need to consolidate this.
There is no way I should have three or four different messaging apps to talk to different people.
So basically started off with implementing Slack across the whole organization.
And then I think the next big project was the knowledge base.
So just like everything else, fragmented.
So I prefer Notion.
So we went with Notion and then we basically set up a shared knowledge base for the whole company.

And then basically after that, we use that to feed into a knowledge base for the consumer side just to handle any of those like, you know, easy or like normal issues that come up and then basically wrap everything up together.
Okay.
So for the knowledge base, what would be in there?
What kind of things?
Yeah.
So basically anything from like troubleshooting, internal processes.

So, for example, like if I drill down into like the sales aspect of it, because everything is going on credits, it was easier to have a knowledge base that would say like, for example, if a customer is in this area, this is the price that we're looking to.
If you're willing to negotiate, this is your lowest price.
An offer without getting, for example, you know, approval from a manager and then this is how to all come up.
And then also like, you know, stock standard things like, you know, this is how we want to send up the invoices.
This is what it should look like.
And then also for support, you've got like, you know, canned responses.
So if a client has got this issue, here's an easy canned response you could have.
And then we also linked that into Intercom as well so that they could have them saved through and then they could just send those as well.
Okay.
So was the knowledge base for like customer support people or sales or like who was the main consumer of the knowledge base?
All of them.
So the focus for the knowledge base was custom support, the sales and the CSM team.
Okay, got it.
like all the client facing.
What was like, what was your kind of process?
Like how did, because I assume it sounded like it started with No.
Okay.
So how did you, like, walk me through a little bit how you went about it?
Yes, so like you mentioned, was absolutely nothing.

What I've learned in these things is the best way to basically tell something to someone and make them feel like they own the process is to basically sit down with them and have it as if it's their idea.
Yeah.
So what I did was, first of all, I had my own idea of how I should set it up.
So I sat down with all their leads and team members and because at that time, was also quite a small team and like, all right, what do you think you need in this knowledge base?
What are you looking for?
I think that's great because you get the buy in.
You know, if they if they can come in and say that, oh, these were all our ideas, there's nothing stopping them from moving through the implementation.
And I think the best part about that is you stop that issue of you set up a knowledge base, but then knowing.
Using it, you know, people are coming back to you, be like, how do I do this?
How do I do that?
So when you get that buy-in right at the initial phase, it's easier for you to actually just sell it because they know that this is what we set it up for.
So after I got that buy-in, we started off with customer support because I felt like that was the lowest hanging fruit and I think they would get the most value out of it.
So we started off with customer support.
We started off with troubleshooting, so like known issues and how to solve those.
And then we moved over to basically like, you know, like canned responses and how we deal with like refunds and all the help pages.
And then we moved over to the client-facing side so that we could have those help pages properly done.
So a lot of the help pages were a little bit all over the place.
They're not like put into nice little categories.
You'd have to use mostly the search.
Function.
So we set that up nicely.
And then we moved over to the CSM side and the sales side.
So we put those together.
So the CSMs and the sales kind of use the same resources.
How do you make it external facing?
Like what, what, what tool were you using for that?
No, we just did that on the website.

Okay.
Okay.
Exactly.

How you connect it to intercom?

So it's easier for them to go through it.
And then of course, we also use intercom AI to just, you know, to assist with any responses as well, based on what the client is saying and based on our knowledge base.
Okay.
So intercom is reading the notion pages.
so,
Yes.

Is that like an API?
How does that work?

didn't know that.
So how do you keep things up to date?
Like I imagine I always find stuff like changes every week, like the answer changes, pricing changes or whatever.
So how do you keep things current?
Yes.
So which is a big headache in this industry because compliance changes all the time and any compliance change has to flow through.
So what I what I do is we've got a standing monthly review.
So so for anything that is not like that needs to be changed immediately.
So, for example, compliance changes immediately.
Basically, those come through.
We update all the pages and then basically let everyone know that this is the new system.
These are the new compliance rules.
But then I do have a monthly standing with all department heads for us just to review.
So basically what they do is that they've got a reminder on their calendar, go off into your realm, see what needs to be changed, and then get back to me the next day so that the changes can be made.
Okay.
Is there some aspect of customers being like, this wasn't helpful, and then you know it's wrong or something?
Or is it all relying on humans to regularly check it?

So we use that.
So that's just part of the feedback loop.
So just for example, if instructions aren't clear, we can go through and then move through.

You know, what do you think this is?
Someone who's not in the industry, doesn't have an understanding of it.
And then they just like, it takes them five minutes, and then I get the feedback from there.

you use, like, does someone on your marketing team own the website, or are you allowed to, like, update it yourself?

Okay, and be like, I want you to update this page, yeah.

Yeah, no, I think my first question will be to do with the current climate.
you know, I think the, a bit volatile, and how is that looking for growthx as well?
Like, are you concerned?
What are current challenges you guys have?
You froze for a tiny second.
you mean like the, like political, what kind, like what environment, like competitive, political AI, like which?
More like economic climate, because I think like, yeah, and then how does that affect like your growth business plans and the goals that you have for the year?
I think it's too hard to know because this company is only like seven months old.
So it's hard.
Like if I'm building our financial forecast, I wouldn't add.
There's not enough like historical data to know a benchmark to know then like what new things impact us.
I think the bigger threats is just going to be from like the ease of entry into the market for new AI companies.
Like there's so much cash flowing to them and they'll, with very few headcount, you can spin up pretty powerful products now.
So I think that is more of like the risk for us.
Which just means we need to just move super fast and try to build like a technical moat, like something that's not replicable so easily.
So it's kind of like the race against time we're in, I would say.
Okay, so trying to take advantage of the first mover.
Yeah, for sure.
Okay.
And then specifically for this role, like, what are you looking to achieve?
And, you know, like, what kind of timelines are you looking at?
Like, you know, first three months, first six months in the first year.
Like, if I jumped on, like, what are our big goals that we need to achieve?
So we have about 30 clients right now and we'll have 100 by the end of the year.
And then we also have about 30 employees and we'll also be 100 by the end of the year.
So it's just like really massive growth where, like, there's a ton of new people starting.
Like, everyone's new, kind of.
And so we're just feeling that things are breaking and, like, we rely, in my opinion, to heavily.
Tools like Notion, which are like fine for like a first version, but they're not like, they're not great, like automation tools.
And they don't, I don't think they link well to other platforms.
Like, how can we scale our headcount and our client count and like, have really like still have tons of visibility into like what's working and what's not.

So a lot of it's going to be like, building systems, setting up new tools, like how can we automate as much as possible, like using different AI tools of like, how can we, for example, we record every call we do, like, how do we use that information to let us know if customers are at risk.
And like, we'll do some, we'll do some like more standard things.
Like, of course, we should have like CSAT scores or like MPS scores, something to determine the customer health.
But like beyond that, what we, what can we do like behind the scenes to determine if our customers are happy.
Because everybody's, they're one-year contracts and everyone's within one year.
So we just don't know what a normal churn rate should be or expansion rate because it's too early, right?
So we need to like hedge that quite a bit by like really deeply understanding like current customers and if they're happy or not.
And same with sales and the sales pipeline.
So it's going to be a lot of like systems and like sort of, I sort of rev-opsy, but like a little bit more post-sales, like post-sales systems and process, if that answers.
Yeah, no, that definitely does make sense.
So, but then do you already have like a customer success team or is that still handled by the sales team?
They're doing both sides of it.
No, we don't actually have sales.
We have like our CEO does all the sales by himself.
We have like 25 people on post-sales.
It's not really like customer success because it's not...
It's like an agency model, so they're really like running the AI workflows and like presenting it to the customer, like all packaged up every week.
So it's a little bit different than like a normal like customer success where like there's no platform that the client's using.
It really feels like an agency interaction.
So that also poses some other challenges because you can't see if they're like using or how they're using or how much they're like in the platform and things like that.
Okay.

No, it's just evening.

Okay, cool.
Well, it so nice to meet you and I'll chat with Marilia later today and then we'll let you know on next steps and what to expect.
All right.
Thank you so much.
It was a lovely meeting.
